summary: Fixed browser 403 on /v1/responses. Root cause: edge proxy forwarded Origin, Referer, sec-* browser headers to Hermes gateway. Hermes rejected cross-origin requests with 403 (empty body). Fix: strip these headers in hermesEdgeAuthProxy.js forward(). Commands run: railway up. All 19 tests pass + E2E verified with full browser header set → 200 completed response.

narrative: Added header stripping in forward(): delete upstreamHeaders.origin, Origin, referer, Referer, and any key starting with sec- or Sec-

debuggingNarrative.bug: Browser POST to /api/hades/hermes/{profileName}/v1/responses returns 403 with empty body (x-powered-by: Express, content-length: 0). Same token+body from CLI (Invoke-WebRequest, Node.js without extra headers) works fine (200).
reproductionSteps: 1. Open browser devtools, send any chat message → 403 empty body response
2. Replicate with Node.js using ALL browser headers (Origin, Referer, sec-*) → 403
3. Replicate with Node.js WITHOUT browser headers → 200

whatWeTried.1. CORS check: Verified CORS middleware is configured correctly (applies before route handler). Origin is allowed. CORS middleware adds headers. The 403 response has x-powered-by: Express but no CORS headers, suggesting it happens AFTER CORS handling or is not a CORS issue.
whatWeTried.2. Auth token check: Verified JWT is valid (same token works from CLI). The 403 happens AFTER verifyEdgeRequest passes (which validates the JWT).
whatWeTried.3. Profile existence check: Profile exists and gateway is running (session returns 200 with gatewayStatus: running). The 403 happens in the /v1/responses handler, not in session creation.
whatWeTried.4. Body content check: Body is valid JSON ({input: 'hello'}). Same body works from CLI.
whatWeTried.5. Headers replication: Created Node.js script replicating ALL browser headers exactly → reproduced 403. Removed headers one by one → Origin header alone causes 403.
whatWeTried.6. Direct Hermes check: Sent request directly to Hermes gateway (localhost:8657) from test-hermes.mjs without Origin → 200. Adding Origin → 403. Confirmed Hermes HTTP server rejects cross-origin requests.

debuggingNarrative.rootCause: The forward() function in hermesEdgeAuthProxy.js spreads all req.headers into upstreamHeaders and passes them to fetch(). This includes Origin, Referer, and sec-* headers from the browser. When forwarded to Hermes' internal API server (listening on 127.0.0.1:8657), Hermes sees a cross-origin Origin header (https://hades-os-monorepo.vercel.app) and rejects the request with 403.
debuggingNarrative.fix: In forward(), strip browser-specific headers (origin, referer, sec-*) before constructing the upstream request. Only keep essential HTTP headers (content-type, content-length, accept, etc.) and the authorization header (replaced with API server key).

filesChanged.change: Added deletion of upstreamHeaders.origin, .Origin, .referer, .Referer, and loop to delete sec-*/Sec-* prefixed headers

debuggingNarrative.verification: Full E2E test with browser header set (Origin, Referer, User-Agent, Accept, Accept-Language, Sec-Fetch-Dest, Sec-Fetch-Mode, Sec-Fetch-Site) → POST /responses returns 200 with completed response object.
remainingRisks: If Hermes adds CORS validation in the future, the header strip may need to be maintained or updated.
Other browser-specific headers (e.g., sec-ch-ua, sec-ch-ua-platform) are also stripped — no known impact.
The 'No models provided' response is a separate Hermes gateway issue (client needs to specify model in request body), not related to this fix.
